title: DOCKER
content:
Here are the detailed notes on Docker, following the exact flow, words, and examples provided in the sources.

Part 1: Introduction and the Problem Statement

  • Introduction to Docker:

    • Docker is an essential part of the entire development process, especially for working software engineers, full-stack developers, backend developers, or DevOps engineers.
    • The core topics to understand are: What is Docker?, Why do we need Docker?, and How is Docker different from a Virtual Machine?.
    • The goal is to understand Docker commands to see how they are used in practical scenarios.

  • The Problem in the Development Process:

    • Before understanding Docker, it is vital to understand the problems in the development process that Docker was created to solve.
    • The Scenario: Imagine a team working on an application. A new developer joins the team and uses a different system, for example, a Mac operating system,.
    • Manual Setup Issues: To run the application, the new developer must manually install all dependencies (e.g., Node, Redis) to replicate the environment.
    • Specific Problems Encountered:
      1. Manual Error: Since setting up dependencies one by one is a manual process, it is prone to errors,.
      2. Version Mismatch: A developer might install a latest version (e.g., Node version 20 or 21) while the application specifically requires an older version (e.g., Node version 16) to run. This causes the application to fail on the new system,.
      3. OS Command Differences: Commands that work on a Windows system might not work the same way on a Mac system.
    • Scaling Issues: In large teams where many developers are joining, relying on everyone to replicate a local development environment perfectly and hassle-free is generally not possible.

  • Production and Deployment Issues:

    • These problems aren't limited to the local environment. When moving a project to a production server, the same environment must be replicated.
    • This leads to many issues and errors during deployment.
    • This situation gives rise to the classic technical problem: "It works on my machine".

Part 2: The Solution - Docker and Containers

  • What is Docker?:

    • Docker is a platform that helps in creating containers.
    • It is a service used to build, destroy, and update these containers.

  • The Concept of Containers:

    • Instead of installing dependencies one by one, we package the code and all its dependencies into a single unit called a container,.
    • This bundle or package can be shared with fellow developers or deployed directly to production servers,. , share in a standardised form that wont require any long setup ; 
    • container toh hogya ab docker inhi container ko bnane me amdad krta h ;
    • Inside a container, we can pre-set everything: which Node version to use, which Redis version to use, and all necessary tools.
    • Cross-Platform Compatibility: Containers work on any machine, whether a developer is using Mac, Windows, or Linux, without needing to change the system to run that specific container.

  • Properties of Containers:

    1. Portable: They can be easily shared from one machine to another, making it easier to share code and dependencies with the development team,.
    2. Lightweight: They are easy to build, update, and destroy.
    3. same container me bhi or chije install hoskte h 
    4. Isolation: A container is a part of the local machine but is completely separate from it. For example, if a local system has Node version 16, a developer can still run two different applications in two different containers—one using version 16 and another using version 20—on the same local system,.
    5. Error Reduction: By providing a pre-packaged container to the team, developers are saved from the manual errors that occur during individual dependency installation.

Please say "proceed" to continue with the notes on Docker Images, Docker Desktop, and practical commands.



Part 3: Docker Image

  • Definition: A Docker image is not a photo. It is actually an executable file that contains instructions on how a container should be created.
  • Relationship between Image and Container:
    • Using a single image, we can create multiple containers.
    • When we share our project with a development team, we don't actually share the container; we first create a Docker image of the application, share that image, and then each team member builds their own container from it.
    • An image is like a static screenshot or a static snapshot of what the code, dependencies, and local development environment look like.
  • The Class and Object Analogy:
    • The relationship between a Docker image and a container is the same as between a Class and Objects in object-oriented programming.
    • A Class acts as a blueprint for how an object should look, but it doesn't occupy resources or memory level space by itself.
    • The Object is what actually occupies memory; similarly, the Container is an instance of the Docker image.
Part 4: Setting Up Docker
  • Docker Desktop: The easiest way to set up Docker on a local system is by downloading Docker Desktop. It is available for Mac, Windows, and Linux.
  • The Interface: Docker Desktop provides a software interface where we can manage and deal with our images and containers.
  • Docker Hub: Just as we upload code to GitHub, we can upload Docker images to Docker Hub. Other developers or teammates can then download these images to create containers on their own systems.
Part 5: Practical Example - "Hello World"
  • Pulling an Image: To bring an image from the internet (Docker Hub) into our system, we use the docker pull command.
    • Example: docker pull hello-world.
    • After running this, the image appears in the Docker Desktop "Images" tab. These images are generally very light-weight (the hello-world image is only a few KBs).
  • Running a Container: To convert an image into a running container, we use the docker run command.
    • Example: docker run hello-world.
    • Running this command automatically creates and starts a container that prints a message.yeh fast run krke exit hojayega toh running state me nhi dikehga but past runn me dikehga 
  • Container Details: In Docker Desktop, each container has a Container ID, a randomly assigned name, and a status (such as "Running" or "Exited").
    • Running: The processes inside the container are still active.
    • Exited: The container has finished its task and we have exited from it.

Part 6: Internal Working (Docker Daemon)
  • When we run a command, the Docker Client contacts the Docker Daemon.
  • Docker Desktop is the UI (User Interface) we use, but the Docker Daemon is the core part that does all the actual work, like pulling images from Docker Hub and creating containers.
Part 7: Practical Example - Ubuntu (Interactive Mode)
  • Interactive Mode: We can run an image like Ubuntu using the -it flag.
    • Command: docker run -it ubuntu.
    • -it stands for Interactive Mode. This is used when we want to run commands inside the container's terminal.
  • Inside the Container:
    • Once the Ubuntu container is running, the terminal no longer points to the local system but to the root directory of the container.
    • We can run standard Linux commands like ls to see folders or mkdir to create new directories.
    • This shows that the entire development process can shift from the local system into a container, making setup hassle-free. 
  • Stopping a Container: To stop a running container, use the command: docker stop <container_id_or_name>.
Part 8: Docker vs. Virtual Machines (VMs)
  • The Architecture:
    • Every system has Infrastructure (Hardware), an Operating System (OS), a Kernel, and an Application Layer where apps like browsers run.
  • How Docker Works:
    • Docker uses the host operating system's kernel and only virtualizes the application layer.
    • Because it doesn't have its own kernel, Docker is lightweight (sizes are in MBs) and faster.
  • How Virtual Machines Work:
    • A VM virtualizes the entire operating system, including its own kernel and the application layer.
    • This makes VMs very heavy (sizes can reach GBs).

  • Compatibility:
    • VMs are compatible with any system because they carry their own kernel. kisie pe bhi chl skta h koi bhi o slike mac , windowns ,linux ;
    • Docker traditionally requires the host kernel to match.
    • docker specially bna h linux ke liye ,isliye majority jo images dekhte h woh linux based hoti h , windows pe linux wali iamge kese run hoit h iske wjh se hum docker desktop ka use krte h ; docker desktop added a lightwieght hypervisor layer jo internally linux distrubtion use krte h or uspe hi run hroah hota h humara 
    •  However, Docker Desktop solves this by adding a lightweight hypervisor layer (a small Linux VM) that allows users on Windows or Mac to run Linux-based containers seamlessly.
